narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, a lower-middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 25, an undergraduate GPA of 2.60, and plans to attend full-time. The model assessed your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try at 76%, which is high enough that the university rejected your application to prioritize students predicted to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking at the bottom, as it stood out well below what's typical for admitted students. Your LSAT score was the next strongest influence in the same direction, ranking much lower than the average of around 37 seen across applicants. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, performing better relative to the dataset average compared to many others. Your undergraduate GPA also helped a bit, as your 2.60 was somewhat closer to the typical 3.21 than some profiles. Your first-year decile, while low, had a milder push toward failure, and planning to study full-time—like most students at 93%—provided a small favorable boost. Overall, your academic rankings and LSAT were the dominant reasons the model leaned toward rejection, especially since they lag behind what's common for those predicted to pass the bar. This doesn't define your potential, and many in similar circumstances have improved through targeted steps. We recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking tutoring or academic support to raise your future deciles, and exploring programs that strengthen GPAs for underrepresented applicants like yourself. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing forward.
